content.text: if/else and Early Returns (+ Returning null)
React components are plain JavaScript functions. You can use any JavaScript control flow —

, early returns, switch statements — to decide what to render. JSX is not a template language with special conditional syntax: whatever you return from the function is what React renders, and if you return

attrs.alt: Three panels comparing if/else before return (assign JSX to a variable, return it at end), early return (guard clause returning null, then happy path), and ternary/&& inline in JSX
attrs.title: Three Ways to Conditionally Render in React

content.text: The most straightforward approach: compute which JSX to show in a variable, then return it at the end. This works well when the two branches are substantially different or when you need to set up state or local variables differently depending on the condition.
You can also return different JSX directly from inside the
blocks, though this is less common because it can make it harder to see the shared structure:

Early returns let you handle edge cases at the top of the component and then write the happy path without nesting. This pattern — also called a guard clause — keeps the "normal" rendering code at the base level, which is easier to read.
Compare this to the alternative — deeply nested conditionals — to see why guard clauses are preferred:
from a component renders nothing — React skips it entirely. No DOM node is created.
is particularly useful for components that optionally render something. The parent doesn't need to check a condition before rendering the component — the component manages its own visibility.
, React still calls the component function. Any side effects in
still run. If you want to prevent all work, use conditional rendering at the call site instead.
if vs switch for Multiple Conditions
For three or more distinct states, a
statement is often cleaner than chained
When mapping a value to a JSX snippet, an object lookup is often the most concise approach:
Sometimes you want different content inside a shared layout. The pattern is: apply guard clauses first, then return the layout with a variable for the varying part:
Both if/else and the ternary operator (
) render conditionally. Choose based on complexity:
Branches are simple (one element or text)
if chain or switch before return
inside JSX. Use ternary, && operator, or move the condition above the return.
, which React does NOT treat as "render nothing" (it throws in development). Use
, and React renders the text "0". Convert to a boolean first:
is hard to read. Extract into a variable or use if/else instead.

A clean design for components with multiple distinct states is to name those states explicitly and map the current state to a render decision. This avoids scattered boolean flags and keeps all conditional logic in one place:
An important constraint: you cannot call hooks conditionally. Hooks must always be called in the same order on every render. This means you cannot have
— the hook must always be called regardless of the condition. The conditional logic goes after the hooks:

Instead of inlining all conditions in one large component, you can split into separate components and conditionally render them. This is especially useful when the branches have very different behaviour:
Performance: Returning null vs Not Rendering
from a component, React still mounts the component — it calls the function and runs any
hooks. When a parent conditionally renders the component at all —
— React mounts and unmounts the component each time. This distinction matters:
when the component needs to preserve state between hidden/visible cycles (a sidebar that remembers scroll position, a form that keeps draft values). Use
when you want the component to fully reset each time it appears.
A common mistake is using CSS to hide/show elements (
) instead of conditional rendering. CSS hiding keeps the element in the DOM and in the accessibility tree. Screen readers still read hidden content with

, early return) to choose what to render.
Guard clauses (early returns) keep the happy path clean and avoid deep nesting.
renders nothing. Use it in components that optionally display content so the parent doesn't need to guard the render call.
or an object lookup table is cleaner than chained
directly inside JSX — move conditions above the return or use ternary/&&.
